song.about:
In February 2012 Gavra Devi held several thousands in the village of Diyatara spellbound with her rendition of this popular folk song. A young bride pleads with her mother -“let me stay a little longer”- while her husband, death himself, refuses her request. Gavra Devi’s veteran voice carries both humor and lived wisdom, sending ripples of bittersweet joy through her listeners' hearts. 

***

Gavra Devi is a popular folk singer from Bikaner, evidently well-loved by her audiences who call her buaji. "Ever since I lost my eyesight as a little girl, I took to bhajan singing and established these poets and their words firmly in my heart", she says. 

This was the 6th night of the week-long Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, a travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in Feb 2012. The Yatra was organized by Lokayan in Bikaner (www.lokayan.org) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore. 

***

Song Credits:
Vocals & Harmonium: Gavra Devi
Dholak: Immamuddin
Manjiras: Prakash

Translation: Vipul Rikhi

Video Credits:
Cameras: Smriti Chanchani & Aarthi Parthasarathy
Editing: Aarthi Parthasarathy
Sound: Atirek Pandey
Sub-Titling: Nandini Nayyar
Collection: The Kabir Project

Place: Diyatara village, Rajasthan
Date of Recording: February 28, 2012

song.songLyricsOriginal:
कबीर पगड़ा दूर है

सौ कोस सांचे बीच

ज्यों ज्यों पग आगे धरे

मिले अघाड़ी आए

 

म्हाने अबके बचा म्हारी माँ

बटाउ आयो लेवण ने

 

आठ कोठड़ी दस दरवाजा

इन काया रे माय

लुकती छिपती मैं फिरूं

लुकती ने छोड़े अब नाय जी

बटाउ आयो...

 

केवे पावणा सुनो डोकरी

सुन ले म्हारी बात

न्हाय धोय कर करो तैयारी

हो जानी बटाऊ रे लार

बटाउ आयो...

 

केवे डोकरी सुन ले बटाऊ

सुन ले म्हारी बात

म्हारी किन्या आड़ी भोली

अबके तो कर दो गुनाह माफ़

बटाउ आयो...

 

सात भायां री बेन लाडली

कोई ना चाले साथ

रमण खेलण री मन में रह गयी

संग री सहेल्यां रे साथ

बटाउ आयो...

 

 

सावन रा सत्रह गया

आई नवल री तीज

भाई बंधू कुटुंब कबीलो

फेरे सीस पर हाथ

बटाउ आयो...

 

 

 

 

कहें कबीर सुनो भाई साधू

इंये सासरे जाए

इंये सासरे सबने जाणो

भज लो नी गोप्यां वालो तान

बटाउ आयो...
song.songLyricsNotes:
Kabir pagda dur hai  

Sau kos saanche beech  

Jyon jyon pag aage dhare  

Mile aghaadi aaye  

 

Mhaane abke bacha mhaari ma  

Batau aayo levan ne  

 

Aath kothadi das darwaja  

In kaaya re maay  

Lukti chipti main phirun  

Lukti ne chhode ab naaye ji  

Batau aayo...  

 

Kehve paavana suno dokari  

Sun le mhaari baat

Nhaay dhoy kar karo taiyaari  

Ho jaani batau re laar  

Batau aayo...  

 

Kehve dokari sun le batau  

Sun le mhaari baat  

Mhaari kinya aari bholi  

Abke to kar do gunaah maaf  

batau aayo...  

 

Saat bhaayan ri ben laadli  

Koi na chaale saath  

Raman khelan ri mann me rah gayi  

Sang ri saheliyaan re saath  

Batau aayo...  

 

Saaven ra satrah gaya

Aai naval ri teej

Bhai bandhu kutumb kabeela

Phere sees par haath

Batau aayo...

 

Kahe kabir suno bhai saadhu  

Inye saasre jaaye  

Inye saasre sabne jaano  

Bhaj lo ni gopyaan waalo taan  

Batau aayo...
song.songLyricsTranslated:
Kabir, the path is far

a hundred miles between

Those who take steps ahead

??

 

Save me just this time, oh mother!

Your son-in-law has come

to take me away!

 

Eight rooms and ten doors

Make this body-house

I flee, I run, I hide from him

My dratted foe won't let me hide!

My husband has come...

 

Says the guest, listen old mother

Listen to what I say

Get her ready to be on her way

This time she's got to go with me

Your son-in-law has come...

 

Says the mother, listen son-in-law

Listen to what I say

My girl's so young and innocent

Let her go just this once!

The traveller has come...

 

Seven brothers of the darling girl,

None can go along

She's got games still to play

With her dear friends

Your son-in-law has come...

 

Monsoon days have come

The festival of Teej is here

Brothers, friends and family all

Came to bless their girl

My husband has come...

 

Says Kabir, listen oh seekers

Go to this marital home

This marital home will claim us all

Meditate on it, like the gopis 

Your son-in-law has come...

 

 

Translation: Shabnam Virmani

film.english_translation: मेरे दुख से टूटा पहाड़ : दास्तान-ए-सस्सी
film.meta_title: My Sorrow Cracked The Mountain - The Legend of Sasui(Hindi)
film.meta_description: एक वीरान और उजड़े मरुस्थल में, एक औरत अकेली चल रही है - रेगिस्तान की गरजती हवा, जलता हुआ सूरज, चाँद और पहाड़, सब उसकी यात्रा की गवाही देते हैं। जैसे ही हम ससुई के साथ उसके खोए हुए प्रेमी की तलाश में कदम मिलाते हैं, कुछ गहरे सवाल उभरने लगते हैं — क्या तलाश और वेदना आपस में गूँथे हुए हैं? दुख के साथ कैसे जिया जाए? सच्चे मिलन के लिए क्या बलिदान देना पड़ता है? शाह लतीफ़ के कलाम के ज़रिये यह फ़िल्म सिंध की इस सूफ़ी दास्तान का अनुसरण करती है - विरह, तलाश और मिलन की आस की एक अद्भुत यात्रा।
film.second_title: मेरे दुख से टूटा पहाड़ : दास्तान-ए-सस्सी

poem.original_text:
(चलती चाकी देख कर, दिया कबीरा रोय

दो पाटन के बीच में, साबत बचा न कोय)


चाकी चाकी सब कहें, कीली कहे न कोय

जो कीली से लाग रहे, वा को बाल न बीका होय


पहले दोहे के जवाब में कहा जाता है की 'कमाल' ने दूसरा दोहा रचा.
poem.english_transliteration_text:
(Chalti chaaki dekh kar, diya Kabira roye

Do paatan ke beech mein, saabat bacha na koye)


Chaaki chaaki sab kahein, keeli kahe na koy

Jo keeli se laag raha, va ko baal na beeka hoy


(It is said that Kamaal composed the second poem as a response to the first poem by Kabir)
poem.english_translation_text:
(Seeing the mill stones grind

Kabir broke into tears

No one escaped being crushed

Between these two slabs)


Everyone talks of mill stones

No one thinks of the pivot

One who clings to the pivot

Can't be crushed by the slabs


(It is said that Kamaal composed the second poem as a response to the first poem by Kabir)
poem.meta_description:
Chaaki chaaki sab kahein, keeli kahe na koy
Jo keeli se laag raha, va ko baal na beeka hoy

For this poem's translation in English, musical rendition and more, visit the site.

extra_translation_rows.text:
Seeing the grinding grind-stone

Kabira starts to weep.

Between these mighty slabs, 

Oh! who can escape unbroken?


They all whine about the daily grind.

No one mentions the pivot.

The one who clasps the pivot

will be the unbroken one!


(It is said that Kamaal composed the second poem as a response to the first poem by Kabir)

[{"category":"video","title":"Bhavsaagar Ke Paar ","second_title":"Naav mein bhaar","content":"As the first step in the Shabad Shaala journey, teachers come together in immersive workshops to engage deeply with the spirit of Kabir's poetry. In this moving session psychologist Sadia Saeed worked with the film 'Bhavsaagar Ke Paar' and held space for the teachers to reflect on the question, 'What is creating the burden in my boat?' 

For the gathered teachers it became a vulnerable encounter with their inner selves, an entry into the spirit of Kabir not only through a pedagogic but a deeply personal lens. We upload here an edited version of this session designed as a video tool for use in inter-personal group settings along with the film    • Bhavsaagar Ke Paar  ., with the hope that it will be of use to many others. 

~

Shabad Shaala is an 8-month curriculum that brings the wisdom of mystic poetry and folk music into 38 schools and nonformal classrooms, inviting children and adults alike to experience the songs of saint-poets and be deeply moved by them. Contact: shabadshaala@gmail.com. 

~

popup_items.content:
As the first step in the Shabad Shaala journey, teachers come together in immersive workshops to engage deeply with the spirit of Kabir's poetry. In this moving session psychologist Sadia Saeed worked with the film 'Bhavsaagar Ke Paar' and held space for the teachers to reflect on the question, 'What is creating the burden in my boat?' 

For the gathered teachers it became a vulnerable encounter with their inner selves, an entry into the spirit of Kabir not only through a pedagogic but a deeply personal lens. We upload here an edited version of this session designed as a video tool for use in inter-personal group settings along with the film    • Bhavsaagar Ke Paar  ., with the hope that it will be of use to many others. 

~

Shabad Shaala is an 8-month curriculum that brings the wisdom of mystic poetry and folk music into 38 schools and nonformal classrooms, inviting children and adults alike to experience the songs of saint-poets and be deeply moved by them. Contact: shabadshaala@gmail.com. 

~

Film Credits
Camera: Shivam Rastogi, Nirbhay Mishra
Editing: Shubhangi Bansal
Editing Assistance: Shivam Rastogi
Editing Guidance: Shabnam Virmani
Produced by Shabad Dhun Foundation

Recorded at the Teachers' Immersion Workshop of Shabad Shaala, July 2024

popup_items.title: Bhavsaagar Ke Paar
popup_items.content:
As the first step in the Shabad Shaala journey, teachers come together in immersive workshops to engage deeply with the spirit of Kabir's poetry. In this moving session psychologist Sadia Saeed worked with the film 'Bhavsaagar Ke Paar' and held space for the teachers to reflect on the question, 'What is creating the burden in my boat?' 

For the gathered teachers it became a vulnerable encounter with their inner selves, an entry into the spirit of Kabir not only through a pedagogic but a deeply personal lens. We upload here an edited version of this session designed as a video tool for use in inter-personal group settings along with the film    • Bhavsaagar Ke Paar  ., with the hope that it will be of use to many others. 

~

Shabad Shaala is an 8-month curriculum that brings the wisdom of mystic poetry and folk music into 38 schools and nonformal classrooms, inviting children and adults alike to experience the songs of saint-poets and be deeply moved by them. Contact: shabadshaala@gmail.com. 

~

Film Credits
Camera: Shivam Rastogi, Nirbhay Mishra
Editing: Shubhangi Bansal
Editing Assistance: Shivam Rastogi
Editing Guidance: Shabnam Virmani
Produced by Shabad Dhun Foundation

Recorded at the Teachers' Immersion Workshop of Shabad Shaala, July 2024
Navjivan Renewal Centre, New Delhi

sample.about:
In February 2012 Gavra Devi held several thousands in the village of Diyatara spellbound with her rendition of this popular folk song. A young bride pleads with her mother -“let me stay a little longer”- while her husband, death himself, refuses her request. Gavra Devi’s veteran voice carries both humor and lived wisdom, sending ripples of bittersweet joy through her listeners' hearts. 

***

Gavra Devi is a popular folk singer from Bikaner, evidently well-loved by her audiences who call her buaji. "Ever since I lost my eyesight as a little girl, I took to bhajan singing and established these poets and their words firmly in my heart", she says. 

This was the 6th night of the week-long Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, a travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in Feb 2012. The Yatra was organized by Lokayan in Bikaner (www.lokayan.org) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore. 

***

Song Credits:
Vocals & Harmonium: Gavra Devi
Dholak: Immamuddin
Manjiras: Prakash

Translation: Vipul Rikhi

Video Credits:
Cameras: Smriti Chanchani & Aarthi Parthasarathy
Editing: Aarthi Parthasarathy
Sound: Atirek Pandey
Sub-Titling: Nandini Nayyar
Collection: The Kabir Project

Place: Diyatara village, Rajasthan
Date of Recording: February 28, 2012

sample.songLyricsOriginal:
कबीर पगड़ा दूर है

सौ कोस सांचे बीच

ज्यों ज्यों पग आगे धरे

मिले अघाड़ी आए

 

म्हाने अबके बचा म्हारी माँ

बटाउ आयो लेवण ने

 

आठ कोठड़ी दस दरवाजा

इन काया रे माय

लुकती छिपती मैं फिरूं

लुकती ने छोड़े अब नाय जी

बटाउ आयो...

 

केवे पावणा सुनो डोकरी

सुन ले म्हारी बात

न्हाय धोय कर करो तैयारी

हो जानी बटाऊ रे लार

बटाउ आयो...

 

केवे डोकरी सुन ले बटाऊ

सुन ले म्हारी बात

म्हारी किन्या आड़ी भोली

अबके तो कर दो गुनाह माफ़

बटाउ आयो...

 

सात भायां री बेन लाडली

कोई ना चाले साथ

रमण खेलण री मन में रह गयी

संग री सहेल्यां रे साथ

बटाउ आयो...

 

 

सावन रा सत्रह गया

आई नवल री तीज

भाई बंधू कुटुंब कबीलो

फेरे सीस पर हाथ

बटाउ आयो...

 

 

 

 

कहें कबीर सुनो भाई साधू

इंये सासरे जाए

इंये सासरे सबने जाणो

भज लो नी गोप्यां वालो तान

बटाउ आयो...
sample.songLyricsNotes:
Kabir pagda dur hai  

Sau kos saanche beech  

Jyon jyon pag aage dhare  

Mile aghaadi aaye  

 

Mhaane abke bacha mhaari ma  

Batau aayo levan ne  

 

Aath kothadi das darwaja  

In kaaya re maay  

Lukti chipti main phirun  

Lukti ne chhode ab naaye ji  

Batau aayo...  

 

Kehve paavana suno dokari  

Sun le mhaari baat

Nhaay dhoy kar karo taiyaari  

Ho jaani batau re laar  

Batau aayo...  

 

Kehve dokari sun le batau  

Sun le mhaari baat  

Mhaari kinya aari bholi  

Abke to kar do gunaah maaf  

batau aayo...  

 

Saat bhaayan ri ben laadli  

Koi na chaale saath  

Raman khelan ri mann me rah gayi  

Sang ri saheliyaan re saath  

Batau aayo...  

 

Saaven ra satrah gaya

Aai naval ri teej

Bhai bandhu kutumb kabeela

Phere sees par haath

Batau aayo...

 

Kahe kabir suno bhai saadhu  

Inye saasre jaaye  

Inye saasre sabne jaano  

Bhaj lo ni gopyaan waalo taan  

Batau aayo...
sample.songLyricsTranslated:
Kabir, the path is far

a hundred miles between

Those who take steps ahead

??

 

Save me just this time, oh mother!

Your son-in-law has come

to take me away!

 

Eight rooms and ten doors

Make this body-house

I flee, I run, I hide from him

My dratted foe won't let me hide!

My husband has come...

 

Says the guest, listen old mother

Listen to what I say

Get her ready to be on her way

This time she's got to go with me

Your son-in-law has come...

 

Says the mother, listen son-in-law

Listen to what I say

My girl's so young and innocent

Let her go just this once!

The traveller has come...

 

Seven brothers of the darling girl,

None can go along

She's got games still to play

With her dear friends

Your son-in-law has come...

 

Monsoon days have come

The festival of Teej is here

Brothers, friends and family all

Came to bless their girl

My husband has come...

 

Says Kabir, listen oh seekers

Go to this marital home

This marital home will claim us all

Meditate on it, like the gopis 

Your son-in-law has come...

 

 

Translation: Shabnam Virmani

data.about:
Kabir's iconic refrain - "I'm drunk on love!" invites you into a state of simple ecstasy. When you carry your beloved within you, you don't have to wait around anymore... union is immediate and constant for such lovers who have shed the burdens of cleverness and tact. This inspired rendition of the popular Kabir song has been composed by Bindhumalini herself. 

***

This song was composed and evocatively rendered by Bindhumalini, accompanied by Vedanth on the guitar in Bikaner during the Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, a travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in February 2012. The Yatra was organized by Lokayan (https://www.rajasthankabiryatra.org/lokayan/) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore. 

Bindhumalini Narayanswamy is a singer and graphic designer based in Bangalore and Vedanth Bharadwaj is an independent composer, singer and guitarist from Chennai. In their music, the rigour of Carnatic and Hindustani classical music comes together with the western sounds of the guitar with great panache and soul! Contact them at bindhumalini@gmail.com & vedanthmusic@gmail.com

data.songLyricsOriginal:
हमन हैं इश्क़ मस्ताना, हमन को होशियारी क्या?

रहें आज़ाद या जग से, हमन दुनिया से यारी क्या?

 

ख़लक़ सब नाम अपने को बहुत कर सिर पटकता है

हमन गुरु नाम साचा है, हमन दुनिया से यारी क्या?

 

न पल बिछड़े पिया से, न हम बिछड़े प्यारे से

इन्हीं से नेह लागी है, हमन को बेक़रारी क्या?

 

कबीरा इश्क़ का माता, दुई को दूर कर दिल से

जो चलना राह नाज़ुक है, हमन सिर बोझ भारी क्या?
data.songLyricsNotes:
Haman hain ishq mastaana, haman ko hoshiyaari kya?

Rahein aazaad ya jag se, haman duniya se yaari kya?

 

Khalak sab naam apne ko bahut kar sir patakta hai

Haman guru naam saacha hai, haman duniya se yaari kya?

 

Na pal bichhade piya ham se, na ham bichhade pyaare se

Inhi se neh laagi hai, haman ko bekaraari kya?

 

Kabira ishq ka maata, dui ko door kar dil se

Jo chalna raah naazuk hai, haman sir bojh bhaari kya?
data.songLyricsTranslated:
I’m drunk on love

Why be clever anymore?

Free of worldliness

Why be attached anymore?

 

The world takes on names,

Multiplies and grows confused

My guru’s name is the truth,

Why be attached anymore?

 

Not once did I lose sight of him

Not once did he leave my side

My heart’s strings are tied to him

I don’t toss and turn anymore

 

Kabir, get drunk on love

Rid the heart of duality

Such a delicate path to tread

Why lug a heavy load anymore?

 

Translation: Vipul Rikhi

data.songtitletraan: My Mind Rejoices in Sacred Simplicity
data.songLyricsOriginal:
कबीर खड़ा बाज़ार में, सब कूं मांगे खैर

नहीं किसी से दोस्ती, नहीं किसी से बैर

 

भव भंजन दुख हरण अमर करण शरीर

आद जुगादी आप हो, सत नाम कबीर

 

कबीर जा जहाँ बैठने गया समुन्दर पार

जती जल की माछली रटत नाम कबीर

 

जो सुख पायो राम भजन में

सो सुख है नहीं अमीरी मां

मेरो मन लागो राम फ़कीरी मां

फ़कीरी मां, गरीबी मां

 

भली रे बुरी, सब सुण लीजे

कर गुजराण गरीबी मां

म्हारो मन लागो...

 

एक दिन तन तोरो खाख मिलेगो

क्यूँ रे फिरे मगरूरी मां?

म्हारो मन लागो...

 

 

 

कहत कबीर सुनो रे भाई साधू

साहिब मिले रे हजूरी मां

म्हारो मन लागो...
data.songLyricsTranslated:
Kabir stands in the marketplace, wishing good to all

No one’s special enemy, he’s no one’s special pal

 

Destroyer of illusions, remover of sorrows

From the beginning of time

You are the true name, says Kabir

 

Wherever Kabir went to sit, there, he crossed over

???

 

The joy of Raam remembrance

Cannot be found in plenty

 

My mind rejoices in sacred simplicity

My mind has taken to living freely

 

On wandering free

In the joys of penury

My mind rejoices...

 

Praise and blame, listen to it all

But don’t stray from simplicity

My mind rejoices...

 

Your body will bite the dust one day

Why strut about, so smug, so vain?

My mind rejoices...

 

Says Kabir, listen seekers

The Lord is found in presence

My mind rejoices...

data.about:
Drop your false pretenses! The intoxicating rhythm of Amolak Ram's song urges you to be a true fakir - to really shed the burden of performing - whether they are religious rites, status symbols or displays of great knowledge. Be humble and authentic in your practice, if you hope to make it beyond this world of delusions. Be a true fakir.

***

In 2011 we invited Mahesha Ram and his troupe to Bangalore for a 4-day ‘Learning with Kabir’ workshop designed as an immersive retreat for educators and artists. Mahesha ji and his mandali would draw the participants into the spirit of satsang in the early mornings and late evenings. The rest of the day, some of us sat with him and recorded new and old songs from his very deep repertoire.

 

One day Mahesha ji handed the tambura to his accompanist Amolak Ram, urging him to sing. This is what he sang, while Mahesha ji himself played the manjiras for him and swayed to the song with unconcealed joy. Amolak Ram ji belongs to Nagaraja village in Jaisalmer, Rajasthan, and has been singing songs of Kabir, Rohal Fakir, Mirabai and other poets from a very young age. He learnt in the immersive satsang tradition of the villages, imbibing these songs from his brothers and uncles. Amolak Ram ji and can be contacted at +91 – 97995-92823 or 91-75681-97423.

***

बड़ले हींडो मांडियो रे

तळे लगाई आग

 

 

 

लटको छोड़ दे रे, जोगिया जीया

जट्टां बढ़ाया हरि नहीं मिले रे

मूंड मुंडाया हरि नहीं मिले रे

मूंड मुंडावे घैटो घेटकी रे

कैसे अमरापुर जाय?

लटको छोड़ दे...

 

न्हाया-धोया हरि नहीं मिले रे  

हर कोई लेवे न्हाय

न्ह्यवे बन रा माछला रे

कैसे अमरापुर जाय?

लटको छोड़ दे...

 

कहयो कबीर धरमीदास नौ रे

वो ही अमरापुर जाए

लटको छोड़ दे...
data.songLyricsNotes:
Badale heendo maandiyo re

Tale lagaai aag

 

Latko chhod de re jogiyaa jiya

Asal fakiri dhaar

 

Jattaan badhaaya Hari nahi mile

Har koi leve badhaay

Jattaan badhaave ban ro reechhdo re

Kaise amraapur jaye?

Latko chhod de...

 

Moond mundaaya Hari nahi mile re 

Har koi leve mundaay

Moond mundaave gheto ghetki re

Kaise amraapur jaye?

Latko chhod de...

 

Nhaaya-dhoya Hari nahi mile re

Har koi leve nhaaye

Nhyave ban ra maachhala re

Kaise amraapur jaye?

Latko chhod de...

 

Kahyo Kabir Dharmidaas no re

Vo hi amraapur jaye

Latko chhod de...
data.songLyricsTranslated:
You hung a nice swing on a banyan branch

But there’s a fire under your butt!

 

 

 

Leave your charades and fake postures, O yogi

Be true in your practice

Be a real fakir

 

Hari isn’t found by growing dreadlocks

It’s easy to grow hair!

Bears in forests have thick hair

Do they make it to heaven?

Leave your charades...

 

Hari isn’t found by shaving your head

Anyone can wield a razor!

Sheep get sheared often

Do they make it to heaven?

Leave your charades...

 

Hari isn’t found by taking dips

It’s easy to take baths!

Fish swim about in holy rivers

Do they make it to heaven?

Leave your charades...

 

Kabir says to Dharmidas

Only a true fakir makes it to heaven

Leave your charades...

 

 

Translation: Vipul Rikhi

data.about:
This gentle, beautiful song brims with emotion and gratitude to the guru who gives knowledge like a healing herb or a potent root, full of the precious nectar of insight. Planting this root in the soil of our consciousness, we tend it carefully to cultivate our own wisdom, and it grows into ​a tall tree​ that ​bursts with flowers and fruits, ​offering us precious shade​ in difficult times. 

Babulal Ranaji lives in a small village called Balgadh near Dewas in Madhya Pradesh, and sings the nirguni bhajans of Kabir, Gorakh Nath and other poets of the Nath tradition. The simplicity of his song accompanied only by the single-stringed ektara recalls the kind of song sung earlier in the Nath Panthi traditions of this region. Babulal Ranaji got inducted into singing from a young age by absorbing these songs from his guru Suraj Nathji Maharaj. He continues an unbroken tradition of satsang in his small ashram in Balgadh since 1998. He can be contacted at 91-9907092222
 

Translation - Shabnam Virmani

ज्ञान की जड़ियां दई सतगुरु जी ने

ज्ञान की जड़ियां दई

 

वा जड़ियां तो म्हाने प्यारी हो लागे 

अमृत रस से भरी

गुराजी ने ज्ञान की जड़ियां दई

 

काया नगर एक, घर एक बंगला

जा बीच गुप्त धरी

गुराजी ने... 

 

पांच नाग, पच्चीस नागिनी 

सूंघत तुरंत मरी

गुराजी ने... 

 

आणि काली ने गुरु सब जग खाया

सदगुरु देख डरी

गुराजी ने... 

 

सत के शरणे शिवगुरु जी बोले 

लइ परिवार तिरी

गुराजी ने...
data.songLyricsNotes:
Gyaan ji jadiyaan dai satguru ji ne

Gyaan ki jadiyaan dai

 

Va jadiyaan to mhaane pyaari ho laage

Amrit ras se bhari

Guraaji ne gyaan ki jadiyaan dai

 

Kaaya nagar ek, ghar ek bangla

Ja beech gupt dhari

Guraaji ne...

 

Paanch naag, pachees naagini

Soonghat turant mari

Guraaji ne...

 

Aani Kaali ne guru sab jag khaaya

Sadguru dekh dari

Guraaji ne...

 

Satt ka sharane Shivguru ji bole

Le parivaar tiri

Guraaji ne...
data.songLyricsTranslated:
Roots of wisdom –

my true guru gave me

healing herbs of wisdom

 

Those roots are so dear to me,

filled with nectar

My guru gave me the roots of wisdom

 

In the city of my body,

a bungalow

In the middle of the bungalow,

a secret

My guru gave me...

 

 

 

Five male snakes

Twenty-five female snakes

smelt those roots

and died instantly

My guru gave me...

 

A fierce goddess

who ate the whole world,

saw my true guru

and trembled

My guru gave me...

 

Taking refuge in truth,

Shivguru says,

I cross over

with my whole family

My guru gave me...

 

Translation: Shabnam Virmani

इक दिन गया मैं इश्क़ दे महकमे
इश्क़ का ज़र्फ़ आज़मा तो सही
घनघोर टकोर से मोर छुपे नहीं
जो कुछ किया सो तुम किया
तू दिल में तो आता है
दीन धरम दी लोड़ न काईं
मरन कनु मैनु हटक ना मुल्ला
मस्जिद ढा दे, मंदिर ढा दे
माई नी मैं रांझा ढूंढ़न चली
मीरा कहे हूँ राणा तने न वरूं
मेहंदी के पत्तों में लाली है
यह तो घर है प्रेम का
रग रग में बोले राम जी
सब आया एक ही घाट से
साध कहे सगराम, हाथ में हीरो आयो
होर की मंगणा मैं रब कोलूं

sample.title: Words knock at the door of a truth that lies beyond them.
sample.thumbnail_excerpt: Kabir and Nanak are seekers of the Word. Their path, if they have one, is the path of the Word.
sample.interview_about:
“In one sense, Kabir and Nanak are seekers of the Word. Their path, if they have one, is the path of the Word. And the Word is the path by which they bring us to that door, and even knock on it. But before that Truth, that light, can show its face, we are blinded by its brilliance, and we shut our eyes.” – Krishna Nath

Born in 1934 in Varanasi in a family of freedom fighters, Krishna Nathji graduated in economics from Kashi Vidyapith. Subsequently he got deeply involved in socialist movements in newly-independent India, even being imprisoned for his activism on several occasions. At some point, Krishnanathji was deeply attracted to the practice of Buddhist meditation (vipassana), thought and literature, and made a close study of Nagarjuna’s and Vajrayana philosophies. He was one of the Buddhist scholars to have held dialogues with the well-known thinker J Krishnamurti. Author of several books, notably on life and traditions in the Himalayas, he had also edited prestigious journals such as ‘Kalpana’, ‘Mankind’ and ‘Aarthiki’ at different times. Krishnathji used to divide his time between his ‘kuti’ (cottage) near Sarnath and his second home, the Krishnamurti Study Centre at Haridvanam in Bangalore. Krishna Nathji passed away on September 06, 2015

sample.profile:
Abdul Hussain Abdullah Turk (1950-2015), or 'Bawa Bha' as he was fondly called in Kutch, Gujarat, was a man whose heart was steeped in the poetry of the famous 17th century Sufi poet Shah Abdul Latif Bhitai and the sounds of the Kutchi Sindhi raag. He not only understood the nuances of poetry and music of the Sindh region intimately, but also shared it with his audiences in a manner infused with the message of peace, oneness and humanity.


'Bawa Bha' was born on 7th September, 1950, in a family of 10 siblings in a small village called Dhrab near Mundra in Kutch, where he lived till the end of his life. He studied formally upto 7th standard in Mundra's government primary school. It was with his uncle, Ali Mamad Jamadar, a master of the 36 Kutchi raag-raaginis, that Bawa Bha came into his own. Together, they would spend hours at the Rasa Peer dargah near Dhrab immersed in singing beyts, kaafis and raags. A story goes that, somewhere near the dargah, there lived a fakir who Bawa Bha became friends with. He held great knowledge about Sufi shrines, ideas and history. For eight long years Bawa Bha traveled the length and breadth of Kutch with him, visiting several Sufi shrines, until one day the fakir left for Uttar Pradesh.


His uncle put Bawa Bha to work in his fish export business where he handled accounts. Even while working, music stayed with him. He started singing at the young age of 10 and remained completely absorbed in Sufi thought and poetry ever since. The famous Sufi singer of Kutch, Sayyad Kasmasa Abha Miyan, once visited Bawa Bha's village to sing at a wedding. The young Bawa Bha presented his songs in front of the master, and that became his first ‘public performance’. He went on to learn Sindhi music and poetry from Abha Miyan. 


Over the years, his popularity grew, spreading across Kutch. He could not only sing beautifully, but through his renditions also interpret the meanings and the ideas of Sufi poetry for the audience, in clear uncomplicated terms. He played a critical role in reviving the values of Sindhi culture and music in the hearts of his audiences. His discourses on the Kutchi way of life were not limited to music alone. From folk legends to history, Sufism to science, literature to nature, this raconteur could interpret anything for his audience and engage them for hours. His ever-spinning yarn of stories could take you to the depths of your own heart, lift you to great heights, make you fall into the lap of nature, take you on travels to the shrines of Sufi saints or leave you at the door of your Beloved.


Bawa Bha's performance at the 1969 festival called ‘Kutchi Raag Ji Roshnai’, organised by Swami Ramsanghji Rathod, was an important milestone. Later in 1982, Bawa Bha himself organised a festival called ‘Kutch Maldhari Lok Kala Mahotsav’ which brought together folk artists of Khadeer, Banni Pachham, Waagad, Anjaar and Bhuj regions of Kutch. Through the Indian National Theatre he also engaged in research on adivasi (indigenous) song, dance and music, during their three day festival in Chandrapur, Maharashtra. Here he presented his discourse on history in the presence of eminent writers like Kaka Saheb Kalelkar and Makarand Bhai. For his contribution to music, he was awarded the Taramati Award.


Sufism for him was a path towards peace, humanity and brotherhood. He organised the first-ever felicitation of talented artists of Kutch at the Urs (death anniversary) of the famous Sufi saint Haji Peer Baba, and this event subsequently became a lasting tradition. He founded a charitable organisation called Khuddam Sufiyat, which works for the betterment of the society and furthering of Sufi values.


Through his association with All India Radio and other organisations, he helped build a pool of knowledge about the region of Kutch. The Kutchi community, their way of life, music, history, legends, eminent personalities, birds and animals and the unique natural ecosystem of Kutch - Bawa Bha became a channel for this cultural knowledge to travel and spread among different people and places. A guru to several Kutchi singers, he honed their skill and vision while they lived and practiced their art with him for years.


For Bawa Bha himself, it is said that the grace of music came after long tireless days of practice at Rasa Peer's dargah. He passed away in 2015, after four long years of struggling with cancer. The people of Kutch remember Bawa Bha as Ghazi, the fighter.


Adapted and translated by Prashant Parvataneni from a Hindi profile written by Mazharuddin Mutwa & Asif Rayama

sample.english_translation: मेरे दुख से टूटा पहाड़ : दास्तान-ए-सस्सी
sample.meta_title: My Sorrow Cracked The Mountain - The Legend of Sasui(Hindi)
sample.meta_description: एक वीरान और उजड़े मरुस्थल में, एक औरत अकेली चल रही है - रेगिस्तान की गरजती हवा, जलता हुआ सूरज, चाँद और पहाड़, सब उसकी यात्रा की गवाही देते हैं। जैसे ही हम ससुई के साथ उसके खोए हुए प्रेमी की तलाश में कदम मिलाते हैं, कुछ गहरे सवाल उभरने लगते हैं — क्या तलाश और वेदना आपस में गूँथे हुए हैं? दुख के साथ कैसे जिया जाए? सच्चे मिलन के लिए क्या बलिदान देना पड़ता है? शाह लतीफ़ के कलाम के ज़रिये यह फ़िल्म सिंध की इस सूफ़ी दास्तान का अनुसरण करती है - विरह, तलाश और मिलन की आस की एक अद्भुत यात्रा।

data.description: यह फ़िल्म 18 वी सदी के एक मशहूर सूफ़ी कवि शाह अब्दुल लतीफ़ के कलाम से प्रेरित है, जिन्हें सिंधी भाषा का सबसे प्रमुख कवि माना जाता है।
data.about_text: यह फ़िल्म 18 वी सदी के एक मशहूर सूफ़ी कवि शाह अब्दुल लतीफ़ के कलाम से प्रेरित है, जिन्हें सिंधी भाषा का सबसे प्रमुख कवि माना जाता है। उनका सारा कलाम 'शाह जो रिसालो' नामक संकलन में शामिल है जिसमें 30 'सुर' (कविता और उनसे जुड़े राग) हैं, जो सिंध के इतिहास, लोक कहानियों और मशहूर प्रेम दास्तानों पर आधारित हैं। उनका कलाम आम लोगों के जीवन को भी उजागर करता है, जैसे बुनकर, लोहार, कुम्हार, मछुआरे आदि। मालूम पड़ता है कि शाह लतीफ़ सिंध की धरती और लोगों के जीवन में पूरी तरह रचे बसे थे और आज भी…

data.description: Bringing to life a Sufi tale from Sindh through the compelling verses of Shah Latif, this film traces an epic journey of separation, awakening, and pursuit.
data.profile_text: Bringing to life a Sufi tale from Sindh through the compelling verses of Shah Latif, this film traces an epic journey of separation, awakening, and pursuit. As we fall in step with Sasui on her quest for a lost beloved, piercing questions begin to arise - Are seeking and sorrow entwined? How does one abide with suffering? What is the sacrifice that union demands?
data.about_text: Across a landscape of loss and desolation, a woman walks alone, as the howling desert wind, the scorching sun, the moon and mountain, bear witness. As we fall in step with Sasui on her quest for a lost beloved, piercing questions begin to arise - Are seeking and sorrow entwined? How does one abide with suffering? data.about: Across a landscape of loss and desolation, a woman walks alone, as the howling desert wind, the scorching sun, the moon and mountain, bear witness. As we fall in step with Sasui on her quest for a lost beloved, piercing questions begin to arise - Are seeking and sorrow entwined? How does one abide with suffering? What is the sacrifice that union demands? Bringing to life a Sufi tale from Sindh through the compelling verses of Shah Latif, this film traces an epic journey of separation, awakening, and pursuit.

songs.songtitletraan: My Mind Rejoices in Sacred Simplicity
songs.songLyricsOriginal:
कबीर खड़ा बाज़ार में, सब कूं मांगे खैर

नहीं किसी से दोस्ती, नहीं किसी से बैर

 

भव भंजन दुख हरण अमर करण शरीर

आद जुगादी आप हो, सत नाम कबीर

 

कबीर जा जहाँ बैठने गया समुन्दर पार

जती जल की माछली रटत नाम कबीर

 

जो सुख पायो राम भजन में

सो सुख है नहीं अमीरी मां

मेरो मन लागो राम फ़कीरी मां

फ़कीरी मां, गरीबी मां

 

भली रे बुरी, सब सुण लीजे

कर गुजराण गरीबी मां

म्हारो मन लागो...

 

एक दिन तन तोरो खाख मिलेगो

क्यूँ रे फिरे मगरूरी मां?

म्हारो मन लागो...

 

 

 

कहत कबीर सुनो रे भाई साधू

साहिब मिले रे हजूरी मां

म्हारो मन लागो...
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Kabir stands in the marketplace, wishing good to all

No one’s special enemy, he’s no one’s special pal

 

Destroyer of illusions, remover of sorrows

From the beginning of time

You are the true name, says Kabir

 

Wherever Kabir went to sit, there, he crossed over

???

 

The joy of Raam remembrance

Cannot be found in plenty

 

My mind rejoices in sacred simplicity

My mind has taken to living freely

 

On wandering free

In the joys of penury

My mind rejoices...

 

Praise and blame, listen to it all

But don’t stray from simplicity

My mind rejoices...

 

Your body will bite the dust one day

Why strut about, so smug, so vain?

My mind rejoices...

 

Says Kabir, listen seekers

The Lord is found in presence

My mind rejoices...

songs.about:
Drop your false pretenses! The intoxicating rhythm of Amolak Ram's song urges you to be a true fakir - to really shed the burden of performing - whether they are religious rites, status symbols or displays of great knowledge. Be humble and authentic in your practice, if you hope to make it beyond this world of delusions. Be a true fakir.

***

In 2011 we invited Mahesha Ram and his troupe to Bangalore for a 4-day ‘Learning with Kabir’ workshop designed as an immersive retreat for educators and artists. Mahesha ji and his mandali would draw the participants into the spirit of satsang in the early mornings and late evenings. The rest of the day, some of us sat with him and recorded new and old songs from his very deep repertoire.

 

One day Mahesha ji handed the tambura to his accompanist Amolak Ram, urging him to sing. This is what he sang, while Mahesha ji himself played the manjiras for him and swayed to the song with unconcealed joy. Amolak Ram ji belongs to Nagaraja village in Jaisalmer, Rajasthan, and has been singing songs of Kabir, Rohal Fakir, Mirabai and other poets from a very young age. He learnt in the immersive satsang tradition of the villages, imbibing these songs from his brothers and uncles. Amolak Ram ji and can be contacted at +91 – 97995-92823 or 91-75681-97423.

***

songs.songLyricsNotes:
Badale heendo maandiyo re

Tale lagaai aag

 

Latko chhod de re jogiyaa jiya

Asal fakiri dhaar

 

Jattaan badhaaya Hari nahi mile

Har koi leve badhaay

Jattaan badhaave ban ro reechhdo re

Kaise amraapur jaye?

Latko chhod de...

 

Moond mundaaya Hari nahi mile re 

Har koi leve mundaay

Moond mundaave gheto ghetki re

Kaise amraapur jaye?

Latko chhod de...

 

Nhaaya-dhoya Hari nahi mile re

Har koi leve nhaaye

Nhyave ban ra maachhala re

Kaise amraapur jaye?

Latko chhod de...

 

Kahyo Kabir Dharmidaas no re

Latko chhod de...
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
You hung a nice swing on a banyan branch

But there’s a fire under your butt!

 

 

 

Leave your charades and fake postures, O yogi

Be true in your practice

Be a real fakir

 

Hari isn’t found by growing dreadlocks

It’s easy to grow hair!

Bears in forests have thick hair

Do they make it to heaven?

Leave your charades...

 

Hari isn’t found by shaving your head

Anyone can wield a razor!

Sheep get sheared often

Do they make it to heaven?

Leave your charades...

 

Hari isn’t found by taking dips

It’s easy to take baths!

Fish swim about in holy rivers

Do they make it to heaven?

Leave your charades...

 

Kabir says to Dharmidas

Only a true fakir makes it to heaven

Leave your charades...

 

 

Translation: Vipul Rikhi

songs.about:
Often sung at the beginning of a 'satsang' to invoke the blessings of Ganesha, this bhajan invites all the gods and goddesses along the qualities they embody, into the temple of the self. The experience of listening to the meditative tunes and hypnotic rhythms of this bhajan could well be compared to the lighting of the incense within.

 

***

 

In 2011 we invited Mahesha Ram and his troupe to Bangalore for a 4-day ‘Learning with Kabir’ workshop designed as an immersive retreat for educators and artists. Mahesha ji would draw the participants into the spirit of satsang in the early mornings and late evenings. Rest of the day, some of us sat with him and recorded new and old songs from his very deep repertoire.

Meditative and mesmeric – these are the qualities of Mahesha Ramji’s music. Unhurried and not in an eager rush to please the public, Mahesha Ramji slows you down, and compels you to step into his rhythm. He belongs to Chhatangadh village near Jaisalmer in Rajasthan, and represents the folk style of the Meghval community, who are the traditional carriers of the poetry of many bhakti poets including Kabir, keeping it alive through a powerful oral tradition. 

 

***

धूप खेवन व्हाला अगरबती निज मंदिरिये मां

रमता पधारो गणपती, ओ जी

 

मूसे री सवारी व्हाला गणपत आया

संग मां रे लाया रिध्धी सिध्धी, ओ जी

 

नंदियेरी सवारी व्हाला शिवजी रे आया

संग मां रे लाया पारवती, ओ जी

 

राम जी रे आया व्हाला लछमण आया

संग मां रे लाया सीता सती, ओ जी

 

बोलिया मीरा जी व्हाला गिरधर गायो रे

गुरु मिल्या रविदासा, ओ जी

 

धूप खेवन व्हाला अगरबती निज मंदिरिये मां

रमता पधारो गणपती, ओ जी
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Dhoop khevan vhaala agarabatti nij mandiriye maan

Ramta padhaaro Ganpati, o ji

 

Moose ri sawaari vhaala Ganpat aaya

Sang maa re laaya Riddhi Siddhi, o ji

 

Nandiye ri sawaari vhaala Shivji re aaya

Sang maa re laaya Parvati, o ji

 

Ram ji re aaya vhaala, Lakshman aaya

Sang maa re laaya Sita Sati, o ji

 

Boliya Meera ji vhaala Giridhar gaayo re

Guru milaya Ravidas, o ji

 

Dhoop khevan vhaala agarabatti nij mandiriye maa

Ramta padhaaro Ganpati, o ji
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Incense sticks in my self-temple are lit...

Stop by on your travels, O Ganesha

 

A mouse for his vehicle, Ganesha arrives...

Together with his wives - wealth, and wisdom

 

Incense sticks in my self-temple are lit...

Stop by on your travels, O Ganesha

 

Riding his bull, Shiva arrives...

Parvati, his partner, by his side

 

Incense sticks in my self-temple are lit...

Stop by on your travels, O Ganesha

 

Raam arrives, and Lakshman arrives...

Together with Sita, embodiment of truth

 

Meera says, in service of Krishna...

I found Ravidas, my true guru

 

Incense sticks in my self-temple are lit...

Stop by on your travels, O Ganesha

songs.about:
In the oral traditions of Kabir and other 'nirgun' mystic poets who evoke the divine as formless, it is not uncommon to have the gathering begin with a 'sagun' bhajan dedicated to Lord Ganesh, believed to be an auspicious deity to invoke at the start of any event. In the spirit of a true satsang, all dualistic impulses to divide or categorise are challenged. This beautiful bhajan prays to Ganesh to leave his high palace, come to this humble gathering and bless it with a sprinkling of colour!


Meditative and mesmeric – these are the qualities of Mahesha Ramji’s music. Unhurried and not in an eager rush to please the public, Mahesha Ramji slows you down, and compels you to step into his rhythm. He belongs to Chhatangadh village near Jaisalmer in Rajasthan, and represents the folk style of the Meghval community, who are the traditional carriers of the poetry of many bhakti poets including Kabir, keeping it alive through a powerful oral tradition.


Mahesha Ramji sang this song along with Bhage Khan and the rest of his troupe, at the Festival of Kabir in Bangalore, 2009. This gathering was organized by the Kabir Project, Srishti School of Art, Design and Technology along with several other partner organizations in Bangalore. It brought together singers from India and Pakistan in an immersive week-long event that included film screenings, live music concerts and talks. Contact Mahesha Ram at +91 99298-76095

Mhaari mandali mein rang barsaavo ji

Elephant-trunked, sorrow-reliever, many-splendoured one

Before all others, invoke Ganesh, the son of Gauri


Primal mother-goddess and giver, Ganesh stays facing her

Come visiting, O elephant-lord

Shower blessings on my community

Sprinkle colour over my gathering

The mouse is your vehicle

Come visiting, O elephant-lord


Saffron mark on the forehead

Let’s offer him some sweets

Come visiting, O elephant-lord


O Tulsidas, colour rains in this way

I lay down my head at his feet

Come visiting, O elephant-lord

songs.about:
Does God take care of the devotee, or does the devotee take care of God? In this moving lullaby, the poet Roopaji Jat asks the all-knowing 'antaryami' to go to sleep and rest a while. Carpenter makes the bed, the gardener brings flowers to be strewn on it, and the poet sings his song, and together they take care of God.


Singer Maheshaji tells us that the poet Roopji Jat was once 'chatting' with Lord Krishna and noticed during the conversation that the god's lids had begun to droop. That's when he tenderly started to sing this lullaby to put his beloved god Krishna to sleep.

सुवी जाओ अंतरयामी रे

थारे नैवणों मां नींद घुराणी रे


किसनो खाती आवे रे

थारे हिंगलू पागारो ढोलियो लावे रे

सुवी जाओ अंतरयामी रे


नेमो छींपो आवे रे

थारे सिरख पथरना लावे रे

सुवी जाओ अंतरयामी रे


फूलां मालन आवे रे

थारे फूलड़ा री सेज बिछावे रे

सुवी जाओ अंतरयामी रे


राधा रूकमण आवे रे

थारे पँखेरी बाव ढूलावे रे

सुवी जाओ अंतरयामी रे


रूपजी जाट गावे रे

सीधा अमरापुर जावे रे

सुवी जाओ अंतरयामी रे
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Suvi jaao antaryaami re

Thaaro naivano maan neend dhuraani re


Kisno khaati aave re

Thaare hingalu paagaaro dhholiyo laave re

Suvi jaao antaryaami re


Nemo chheempo aave re

Thaare sirakh patharana laave re

Suvi jaao antaryaami re


Phoolaan maalan aave re

Thare phoolada ri sej bichhaave re

Suvi jaao antaryaami re


Radha rookman aave re

Thaare pankheri baav dhhoolaave re

Suvi jaao antaryaami re


Roopji jaat gave re

Seedha Amrapur jaave re

Suvi jaao antaryaami re
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Sleep awhile, all-knowing one

Your eyes are heavy with slumber


Kisno, the carpenter, is here

With a bed of sandalwood legs

Nemo and Cheempo are here

With a mattress and a pillow


Phoolan, the gardener, is here

Strewing your bed with flowers


Radha and Rukmini are here

With hand-fans to keep you cool


Roopji Jat sings this song

He’s found the eternal city


Translation: Vipul Rikhi

songs.about:
This song is unusual in that it tells in Kabir’s own voice a popular legend from his life. As Kabir’s popularity and influence grew in Varanasi, the jealous Brahmins plotted to humiliate him. Four of them set out disguised as sages, and invited hundreds of people to a great feast that they said would be given by Kabir. Great crowds gathered at Kabir’s hut on the day of the feast. Kabir himself being a poor weaver could not play host so he ran away to the forest. The day was saved by god Hari himself, who arrived in the guise of many Kabirs, and personally greeted and fed all the guests. 


At one level these legends seem to have little historic value. However, scholar David Lorenzen argues, “Legends do not gather around saints and heroes completely at random…. The usually unknown authors of the legends, whether individual or collective, construct the legends on the basis of their own and their listeners’ or readers’ needs, tastes, hopes and desires.” 


Most of the legends about Kabir flow out of the low-caste, non-Brahmin current of nirgun bhakti. They manifest the ideology of the poor and powerless, and register protest against their exploitation by the rich and powerful. The legends themselves become active historical forces that reinforce the social resistance of the marginalised community that creates and consumes them. 


Meditative and mesmeric – these are the qualities of Mahesha Ram ji’s music. Unhurried and not in an eager rush to please the public, Mahesha Ram ji slows you down, and compels you to step into his rhythm. He belongs to Chhatangadh village near Jaisalmer in Rajasthan, and represents the folk style of the Meghwal community, who are the traditional carriers of the poetry of many bhakti poets including Kabir, keeping it alive through a powerful oral tradition.


Mahesha Ram ji sang this song along with Bhage Khan and the rest of his troupe at the Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, 2012, a week-long travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in February 2012. The Yatra was organised by Lokayan in Bikaner (www.lokayan.org) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore. Contact Mahesha Ram at +91 99298-76095.

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
कबीर कबीर क्या करो?,सोचो आप शरीर

पाँच इंद्रियाँ वश करो, तो आप ही दास कबीर


कबीर तेरी झोंपड़ी, गलकटन के पास

करन्ता सो भरन्ता, तू क्यों फरे उदास?


सांवलिए बिना, कुण रे बंधावे धीर?

गुरांजी बिना, कुण रे बंधावे धीर?


काशी नगरी मां रामा, ब्रपर तेड़ाया

लिख लिख मेलां चीर

बीज तीज रा जीमण आई जो

यज्ञ रचायो कबीर


माया नहीं बाँधी, रामा, पल्ले हमारे

पड़ी भगत मां भीड़

बन खन ढूंढ़या, बन खन भटक्या

व्याकुल भयो शरीर


अनंत कढ़ाईयां, रामा, अनंत भंडारा

अनंत भरयो जल नीर

आप हरि चंद होय बणजारा

बालद करी बहीर


आप रे भगत की, रामा, सहाय करिए

पड़यो समंद में सीर

कहत कबीर सुणो भाई साधो

मैं तेरा मस्त फ़कीर
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Kabir Kabir kya karo, socho aap shareer

Paanch indriyaan vash karo, toh aap hi Das Kabir


Kabir teri jhompadi, gal katan ke paas

Karanta so bharanta, tu kyun phire udaas


Saanvaliye bina, kun re bandhaave dheer?

Guraanji bina, kun re bandhaave dheer?


Kaashi nagri maan Raama, brapar teḍaaya

Likh likh melaan cheer?

Beej teej ra jameen aayi jo

Yagya rachaayo Kabir

 

Maaya nahin baandhun palle hamaare

Padi bhagat maan bheed

Ban khan dhhoondya, ban khan bhatakya

Vyaakul bhayo shareer


Anant kadhaayeeyaan anant bhandaara

Anant bharyo jal neer

Aap hari chand hoy banjaara

Baalad kari baheer


Aap re bhagat ki sahaay kariye

Padyo samand mein sir

Kahat Kabir suno bhai saadho

Main tera mast fakir
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Why chant Kabir's name?

Look into your own self

Master the five senses

And you are Kabir yourself


Kabir, your hut is next to cut-throats and butchers

As they sow, so shall they reap

Why lose your sleep?


Without my Dark One,

who will give me courage?

Without my guru,

who will give me courage?


In Kashi city once,

the sages wrote out invitations

 “On the day of Teej,

come to eat a fine feast –

made by Kabir!”


No money in my pockets.

 The guests grew into crowds.

I hid in the forests,

wandered in the woods –

agitation filled my body!


Countless vessels, unending food,

endless pots of water!

Hari himself, the wandering merchant

brought his cartload of supplies!


Hari himself served the guests,

Kabir’s cup runs over!

Says he, listen oh seekers,

I’m your intoxicated fakir!


Translation: Vidya Rao

songs.about:
मन म्हारो मोयो लाल बैरागी

You bewitched my mind

Coloured it red with detachment


A heady mix of longing, jealousy, complaint, enchantment and non-attachment this song gives us a taste of Meera's fierce love for Krishna - a passion paradoxically steeped in the colours of dispassion or 'bairaag'.


In 2011, we invited Mahesha Ram and his troupe to Bangalore for a 4-day ‘Learning with Kabir’ workshop designed as an immersive retreat for educators and artists. Mahesha ji would draw the participants into the spirit of satsang in the early mornings and late evenings. Rest of the day, some of us sat with him and recorded new and old songs from his very deep repertoire.


Meditative and mesmeric – these are the qualities of Mahesha Ramji’s music. Unhurried and not in an eager rush to please the public, Mahesha Ramji slows you down, and compels you to step into his rhythm. He belongs to Chhatangadh village near Jaisalmer in Rajasthan, and represents the folk style of the Meghval community, who are the traditional carriers of the poetry of many bhakti poets including Kabir, keeping it alive through a powerful oral tradition. Contact Mahesha Ram at +91 99298-76095

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
मन म्हारो मोयो लाल बैरागी

दूजो म्हारे नजरां मां नहीं आवे

कौमनगारो जी कान्ह जी


सैसों गोपियां (मां) राधा पटरानी

कुबुद्धा रे महले क्यों जावे?

कौमनगारो जी…


हिये मंदरिये (मां) आवणो जावणो

नैवणो मां नींद कनु नहीं आवे

कौमनगारो जी…


ताता भोजन थाले परूसियां

हेकलड़ी नो कनु नहीं भावे

कौमनगारो जी…


बन रा बन (मां) धैन चरावे

मुरली रो मोयो पाछो नहीं आवे

कौमनगारो जी…


दासी के ऊपर किरपा जो कीन्ही

ए जस मीराबाई आप गावे

कौमनगारो जी…
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Mann Mhaaro Moyo Laal Bairaagi

Doojo mhaare najra maan nahin aave

Kaumangaaro ji kaanh ji


Saiso gopiyaan (maan) Radha patraani

Kubuddha re mahale kyon jaave?

Kaumangaaro ji…


Hiye mandariye (maan) aavano jaavano

Naivano maan neend kanu nahin aave

Kaumangaaro ji…


Tata bhojan thaale paroosiyaan

Hekladi no kanu nahin bhaave

Kaumangaaro ji…


Ban ra ban (maan) dhain charaave

Murali ro moyo paachho nahin aave

Kaumangaaro ji…


Daasi ke oopar kirpa jo kinhi

E jas Mirabaai aap gave

Kaumangaaro ji…
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
You bewitched my mind

Coloured it red with detachment

Now I don’t see any other

O Krishna, the enchanter


Radha is chief among a thousand gopis

Why visit all the fools’ palaces?

O Krishna, the magician


So much activity in this temple

There is no rest nor sleep for me

O Krishna, the enchanter


I’ve made hot, fresh food for you

No joy in eating by myself

O Krishna, the magician


He tends to cows in countless forests

Those caught by his flute are saved

O Krishna, the enchanter


You’ve showered grace on this slave

Meera is moved to sing of this glory

O Krishna, the magician


Translation: Vipul Rikhi

songs.about:
गहरी नदियां नाव पुरानी, खेवटिया है नादान 

अलख पुरुष तेरे है घट अंदर, खेई के पार लगाई


In a powerful image from this song from Malwa, the creaky boat of our self heaves and lurches in the tumultuous waters of samsara, navigated by an inexperienced boat (god) man. Poet Dharamdas urges us to take the oars and be guided by the unseen spirit within our own selves!


Kaluram Bamaniya belongs to a vibrant tradition of singing bhakti poets such as Kabir, Gorakhnath, Bananath and Meera in the Malwa region of Madhya Pradesh in India. He started learning at the early age of 9, by accompanying his father, grandfather and uncle on the manjiras. When he was 13 years old he ran away from home and went to Rajasthan, where he absorbed a wide repertoire of songs from an itinerant Mirasi singer Ram Niwas Rao for 1-2 years. Contact Kaluramji at +91-9826400632, bairagkabir.bamniya@gmail.com.

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
मत करो रे चतुराई

दिल का दाग दूरा करो भाई

मन का मैल परा करो भाई


उठ परभात झूठ मत बोलो

मत करो निंदा पराई

पाप गठरिया पल्ले रे मत बांधो

निर्फल जावेगा कमाई

रे दिल का दाग दूरा करो भाई


दिल दरियाव समुन्दर नहावो

सुरत (ज्ञान) का साबुन लगाई

सुरत शिला पर पकड़ पछाटो

जहां से होगा उजलाई

रे दिल का दाग दूरा करो भाई


गहरी नदियां नाव पुरानी

खेवटिया है नादान

अलख पुरुष तेरे है घट अंदर

खेई के पार लगाई

रे दिल का दाग दूरा करो भाई


अँधा हो के फिरे मगरूरी

आँख इधर गढ़ जाई

हिवड़ा के माई थारे दिवलो जले है

जां से होए उजलाई

दिल का दाग दूरा करो भाई


तम कई रूठो म्हारा जनम सुधारण

और रूठया से क्या होई

धरमदास की अरज गोसाईं

चरणों में लीजो रखाई

दिल का दाग दूरा करो भाई
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Mat karo re chaturaai

Dil ka daag doora karo bhaai

Mann ka mail para karo bhaai


Uthh parbhaat jhoot mat bolo

Mat karo ninda paraai

Paap gathhariya palle re mat baandho

Nirfal jaavoge kamaai

Dil ka daag doora karo bhaai


Dil dariyaav samundar nahaavo

Surat (gyaan) ka saabun lagaai

Surat shila par pakad pachhaato

Jahaan se hoga ujlaai

Dil ka daag doora karo bhaai


Gahari nadiyaan naav puraani

Khevatiya hai naadaan

Alakh purush tere hai ghat andar

Khei ke paar lagaai

Dil ka daag doora karo bhaai


Aandha ho ke phire magroori

Aankh idhar gadhh jaai

Hivada ke maai thaare divalo jale hai

Jaan se hoye uljaai

Dil ka daag doora karo bhaai


Tam kai rooto mhaara janam sudhaaran

Aur rootya se kya hoi

Daramdaas ki araj gosaain

Charanon mein leejo rakhaai

Dil ka daag doora karo bhaai
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Don’t get so clever, friend

Wipe away the blots on your heart

Scrub away the stains from your mind


Why pass all day in pretence?

Why be so critical of others?

Don’t lug around this bag of crimes

All your gains will go down the drain

Wipe away the blots on your heart


Bathe in the ocean of the heart,

apply the soap of insight 

Bathe in the ocean of the heart,

apply the soap of wisdom

Flog your self

on the stone of awareness

Emerge then, radiant and bright

Scrub away the stains from your mind


The rivers are deep, the boat is old,

and the boatman is naive

The unseen spirit dwells in your body

It will bring the boat to its goal

Wipe away the blots on your heart


You strut about, blinded by pride,

eyes engrossed by the world

There’s a flame alive in your body

which brings clarity of sight

Scrub away the stains from your mind


Why turn away, having sorted out my life 

Think of my plight, if you neglect me

Master, listen to Dharamdas’s plea

Keep me forever close to your feet

Wipe away the blots on your heart


Translated By: Vipul Rikhi

songs.about:
एक लकड़ी तो धूनी में जले 

दूसरा महलों में जड़िया जी 


The wood tossed into a sacred fire, and the wood studded on palace walls - both one, both made by the same master. In this song, Gorakhnath evokes contrasts and abstract images to signal concepts of oneness and awareness. Smoke rises up to the heavens; Gorakh folds his hands in prayer; he has found his place in heaven.


This haunting song is sung by Kaluram Bamaniya, who belongs to a vibrant tradition of singing bhakti poets such as Kabir, Gorakhnath, Bananath and Meera in the Malwa region of Madhya Pradesh in India. He started learning at the early age of 9, by accompanying his father, grandfather and uncle on the manjiras. When he was 13 years old he ran away from home and went to Rajasthan, where he absorbed a wide repertoire of songs from an itinerant Mirasi singer Ram Niwas Rao for 1-2 years. Contact Kaluramji at 91 9826400632 or bairagkabir.bamniya@gmail.com. 

 

Translation: Vipul Rikhi

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
मिलो रे मिलो रे कोई म्हारा देस रा

दो दो बातां करांगां जी 

मिल्या था बोल्या नहीं 

जूना साध कहवाना जी 


लकड़ लकड़ सब एक है

एक ही मालिक ने गढ़िया जी

एक लकड़ी तो धूणी में जले

दूसरी महलां में जड़िया जी 


मिलो रे मिलो कोई म्हारा देस रा 

 वां की बातां करांगां जी 


पत्थर पत्थर सब एक है

उना मालिक ने गढ़िया जी 

एक पत्थर की मूरत बनी

नुगुरो डेली में गढ़ियो जी 

मिलो रे मिलो…


धरती माता रा चूल्हा किया 

आसमान किया है कढ़ेला जी 

चार जुग झोकन दिया

धुंआ गगन समाना जी 

मिलो रे मिलो…


दोई कर जोड़ गोरख बोलिया

पाया वैकुंठ वासा जी

जिन ने रटीया सत नाम को

वोई नर संत सुजाना जी  

(पहुंचा सत धाम में)

मिलो रे मिलो…
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Milo re milo re koi mhaara des ra

Do do baataan karaanga ji

Milya tha bolya nahin

Joona saadh kahvaana ji


Lakad lakad sab ek hai

Ek hi maalik ne gadhhiya ji

Ek lakdi to dhooni mein jale

doosra mahlaan mein jadiya ji

Milo re milo re…


Patthar patthar sab ek hai

Una maalik ne gadhhiya (Ghadiya?) ji

Ek patthar ki moorat bani

Nuguro deli (peri) mein gadhhiyo ji

Milo re milo re…


Dharti maata ra choolha kiya

Aasmaan kiya hai kadhhela ji (tava hai)

Chaar jug jhokan diya

Dhuaan gagan saamna ji

Milo re milo re…


Doi kar jod Gorakh boliya

Paaya vaikunt vaasa ji

Jin ne rateeya sat naam ko

Voi nar sant sujaan ji

(pahuncha sat dhaam mein)

Milo re milo re…
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Oh, to meet someone from my land

And have a few words with him

To meet but not to speak

Such an old kinship


This wood, that wood, they’re all one

The same master made them all

This wood burns in a sacred fire

That one’s studded in a palace wall

Oh, to meet…


This stone, that stone, they’re all one

That master fabricated them both

This stone becomes part of the idol

That one’s on the temple floor

Oh, to meet…


Mother earth, the firewood stove

The sky transforms to a cauldron

All four ages tossed to the flames

The smoke rises up to the heavens

Oh, to meet…


Gorakh folds his hands in prayer

He’s found his place in heaven

One who remembers the true name

Arrives at the true destination

Oh, to meet…


Translation: Vipul Rikhi

songs.about:
In this song Kabir imagines a country beyond all constructs - physical, social or religious. He dismisses even his own saakhi and shabad (couplets and songs) as irrelevant in this country. Such is the country that the guru has revealed in signs for Kabir.


This song could be seen as an echo of several poetic and philosophical expressions where the ultimate truth is indescribable in words and can only be arrived at by a series of negations. In Nirvana Shatakam, Shankara speaks of the vedantic idea of reaching ultimate reality through a series of 'neti-neti' or “not this, not that”. Buddhist philosopher Nagarjuna also describes supreme reality similarly in his verse Paramarthastava. Mystic poet Lal Ded says that Shiva is that which cannot be described. Perhaps all these poets and philosophers are reminding us that the truth we seek always exceeds the grasp of language - 'mukh par kahyo nahin jaaye'.


Meditative and mesmeric – these are the qualities of Mahesha Ramji’s music. Unhurried and not in an eager rush to please the public, Mahesha Ramji slows you down, and compels you to step into his rhythm. He belongs to Chhatangadh village near Jaisalmer in Rajasthan, and represents the folk style of the Meghval community, who are the traditional carriers of the poetry of many bhakti poets including Kabir, keeping it alive through a powerful oral tradition. Contact Mahesha Ram at +91 99298-76095

एवी एवी सेण बताई म्हाने सतगुरु

मुख पर कहयो नहीं जाए, रे साधो


अमारे रे देस मांई ना धरा नहीं गगना

नहीं कोई पवन नहीं पानी, रे साधो

एवी एवी सेण…


हमारे रे देस मांई ना चंदा नहीं सूरज

एवी एवी सेण…


हमारे रे देस मांई ना बाणी नहीं खाणी

नहीं कोई वेद नहीं गीता, रे साधो

एवी एवी सेण…


हमारे रे देस मांई ना ब्रह्मा नहीं विषणू

नहीं कोई शंकर देवा, रे साधो

एवी एवी सेण…


हमारे रे देश में ना मरे नहीं तो जन्मे

नहीं कोई शबद नहीं साखिया, रे साधो

एवी एवी सेण…


मंजले मंजले एक संत जाए पहुंचया

songs.songLyricsNotes:
Evi evi sen bataai mhaane satguru

Mukh par kahyo nahin jaaye, re saadho


Amaare re des maeen na dhara nahin gagana

Nahin koi pavan nahin paani, re saadho

Evi evi sen…


Hamaare re des maeen na chanda nahin sooraj

Nahin koi naulakh taara, re saadho

Evi evi sen…


Hamaare re des maeen na baani nahin khaani

Nahin koi ved nahin geeta, re saadho

Evi evi sen…


Hamaare re des maeen na brahma nahin vishnu

Nahin koi Shankar deva, re saadho

Evi evi sen…


Hamaare re des mein na mare nahin to janme

Nahin koi shabad nahin saakhya, re saadho

Evi evi sen…


Manjale manjale ek sant jaae pahunchya

Kabir sant chadhhya nirvaan, re saadho

Evi evi sen…
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
My true guru gave me

such amazing signs –

such that cannot be told!

 

In my land,

there’s no earth, no sky,

neither wind nor water!

 

In my land,

there's no sun, no moon,

nor the nine lakh stars.

 

In my land,

neither Brahma nor Vishnu,

nor the god Shankar either!

 

In my country,

no verse, no story,

not any Veda or Gita!

 

In my land,

no birth, no death,

no verse, no couplets!

 

Step by step, a saint made it there

Kabir climbed to freedom.


Translation: Vidya Rao

reflections.title: Freedom will be your slave
reflections.meta_description: "Kabir said you won't find God in temples or by taking holy dips in pilgrimage places. Confront yourself the way one looks in a mirror to comb one's hair. Freedom's not something you can get from here or there", says folk singer, Kaluram Bamaniya.

reflections.title: Ulatbaansi discourages your mind from functioning in its normal way
reflections.meta_description: Linda Hess shares her thoughts on Kabir's ulatbaansi (upside down poems). She argues that ulatbaansi is Kabir's way of challenging the machinations of mind and go beyond mental frameworks.

reflections.title: Seeking 'shabd' is not for the half-hearted
reflections.meta_description: "We've to come all the way from Rajasthan to Bangalore. Say, we reach Mumbai or Mysore and then we turn back because- 'oh, this journey is too long, I'm tired of sitting, let me turn back!' Then how will you reach Bangalore?" reflects folk singer, Mahesha Ram. \n\nFor more on this reflection, visit our site.

reflections.title: Turn the prayer beads within
reflections.meta_description: "Say I sit with closed eyes and turn my prayer beads, but all I can think of is if my cow has been milked or what my wife or child might be doing. What use is this meditation?" questions folk singer, Mahesha ram. \n\nFor more on this reflection, visit our site.

films.english_translation: Meera, Who Is Krishna To You After All?
films.about_text: Saint. Lover. Devotee. Rebel. Who was Meera after all? We sense her through her songs. We taste her rage, her love. We feel the power of her radical surrender. And yet Meera, one of the most well-loved saint-poets of India, remains an enigma.

films.about_text: Delving into a Sufi tale about a simple village girl held hostage in the fortress of a powerful king, this film grapples with questions of freedom and imprisonment, longing and belonging. It weaves together the zaahir and the baatin (the apparent and the hidden meanings) of this tale, which is at once spiritual and social, personal and political, historical and timeless, about a woman, her lost homeland and the struggle to remember who we truly are.

films.about_text: Across a landscape of loss and desolation, a woman walks alone, as the howling desert wind, the scorching sun, the moon and mountain, bear witness. As we fall in step with Sasui on her quest for a lost beloved, piercing questions begin to arise - Are seeking and sorrow entwined? How does one abide with suffering? What is the sacrifice that union demands? Bringing to life a Sufi tale from Sindh through the compelling verses of Shah Latif, this film traces an epic journey of separation, awakening, and pursuit.

films.english_translation: Glimpses of the Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, 2012
films.about_text: The desert is an ideal place to slip within, when the eye has almost nothing external to grasp. And so some of us carried out two journeys within this one. We traversed long, lonely desert roads, but also treaded a bit on the dark, frightening path within, aided by the power of song. The ‘marubhumi’ – ‘difficult terrain’ or even ‘land of death’ – is an appropriate setting for a poetry that talks of dying to be born, to live, to be free.'The Journey Home' shares glimpses of the Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, a rambunctious, melodic and feisty travelling festival of…

films.about_text: A journey in search of the “des” (country) invoked in the poetry of Kabir, a 15th century Indian mystic, this film interweaves the stories of two people from two very different countries – Indian folk singer Prahlad Tipanya and North American scholar Linda Hess. Where is Kabir’s country? The answer is elusive, as we journey through song and poem into these two lives, brought together in an unlikely friendship by the cross-cultural resonance of Kabir.

films.about_text: Kabir was a 15th century mystic poet of north India who defied the boundaries between Hindu and Muslim. He had a Muslim name and upbringing, but his poetry repeatedly invokes the widely revered Hindu name for God – Ram. Who is Kabir’s Ram? This film journeys through song and poem into the politics of religion, and finds myriad answers on both sides of the hostile border between India and Pakistan. (103 min | Hindi with Kannada Narration & SubTitles)*Silver Trophy, Best Non-Fiction Film, Indian Documentary Producers' Association Awards, 2009*1st Prize (shared), One Billion Eyes Documentary Film Festival, Aug 2008 (Chennai)See…

films.about_text: Kabir sang his poems in the market place, his spirituality firmly grounded in the public square. 600 years after his time, this 15th century Indian mystic poet’s legacy can be found in both spaces – sacred and secular. This film interweaves the deification of Kabir by a religious sect with his secular appropriation by a social activist group. The story unfolds through the life of Prahlad Tipanya, a Dalit singer whose participation in both domains begins to raise difficult questions for him about ritual and organized religion.

data.about:
In February 2012 Gavra Devi held several thousands in the village of Diyatara spellbound with her rendition of this popular folk song. A young bride pleads with her mother -“let me stay a little longer”- while her husband, death himself, refuses her request. Gavra Devi’s veteran voice carries both humor and lived wisdom, sending ripples of bittersweet joy through her listeners' hearts. 

***

Gavra Devi is a popular folk singer from Bikaner, evidently well-loved by her audiences who call her buaji. "Ever since I lost my eyesight as a little girl, I took to bhajan singing and established these poets and their words firmly in my heart", she says. 

This was the 6th night of the week-long Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, a travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in Feb 2012. The Yatra was organized by Lokayan in Bikaner (www.lokayan.org) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore. 

***

Dholak: Immamuddin

data.songLyricsOriginal:
कबीर पगड़ा दूर है

सौ कोस सांचे बीच

ज्यों ज्यों पग आगे धरे

मिले अघाड़ी आए

 

म्हाने अबके बचा म्हारी माँ

बटाउ आयो लेवण ने

 

आठ कोठड़ी दस दरवाजा

इन काया रे माय

लुकती छिपती मैं फिरूं

लुकती ने छोड़े अब नाय जी

बटाउ आयो...

 

केवे पावणा सुनो डोकरी

सुन ले म्हारी बात

न्हाय धोय कर करो तैयारी

हो जानी बटाऊ रे लार

बटाउ आयो...

 

केवे डोकरी सुन ले बटाऊ

सुन ले म्हारी बात

म्हारी किन्या आड़ी भोली

अबके तो कर दो गुनाह माफ़

बटाउ आयो...

 

सात भायां री बेन लाडली

कोई ना चाले साथ

रमण खेलण री मन में रह गयी

संग री सहेल्यां रे साथ

बटाउ आयो...

 

 

सावन रा सत्रह गया

आई नवल री तीज

भाई बंधू कुटुंब कबीलो

फेरे सीस पर हाथ

बटाउ आयो...

 

 

 

 

कहें कबीर सुनो भाई साधू

इंये सासरे जाए

इंये सासरे सबने जाणो

भज लो नी गोप्यां वालो तान

बटाउ आयो...
data.songLyricsNotes:
Kabir pagda dur hai  

Sau kos saanche beech  

Jyon jyon pag aage dhare  

Mile aghaadi aaye  

 

Mhaane abke bacha mhaari ma  

Batau aayo levan ne  

 

Aath kothadi das darwaja  

In kaaya re maay  

Lukti chipti main phirun  

Lukti ne chhode ab naaye ji  

Batau aayo...  

 

Kehve paavana suno dokari  

Sun le mhaari baat

Nhaay dhoy kar karo taiyaari  

Ho jaani batau re laar  

Batau aayo...  

 

Kehve dokari sun le batau  

Sun le mhaari baat  

Mhaari kinya aari bholi  

Abke to kar do gunaah maaf  

batau aayo...  

 

Saat bhaayan ri ben laadli  

Koi na chaale saath  

Raman khelan ri mann me rah gayi  

Sang ri saheliyaan re saath  

Batau aayo...  

 

Saaven ra satrah gaya

Aai naval ri teej

Bhai bandhu kutumb kabeela

Phere sees par haath

Batau aayo...

 

Kahe kabir suno bhai saadhu  

Inye saasre jaaye  

Inye saasre sabne jaano  

Bhaj lo ni gopyaan waalo taan  

Batau aayo...
data.songLyricsTranslated:
कबीर, रास्ता बहुत दूर है

के बीच सौ मील

कदम आगे बढ़ाने वाले

<पी शैली='पाठ-संरेखण:केंद्र'>??

 

इस बार मुझे बचा लो, हे मां!

आपका दामाद आ गया है

मुझे दूर ले जाने के लिए!

 

आठ कमरे और दस दरवाजे

यह बॉडी-हाउस बनाएं

मैं भागता हूं, मैं भागता हूं, मैं उससे छिपता हूं

मेरा घिसा-पिटा दुश्मन मुझे छिपने नहीं देगा!

मेरे पति आये हैं...

 

मेहमान कहता है, सुनो बूढ़ी मां

मैं जो कहता हूं उसे सुनो

उसे अपने रास्ते पर चलने के लिए तैयार करें

इस बार उसे मेरे साथ जाना होगा

आपका दामाद आया है...

 

कहती है मां, सुनो दामाद जी

मैं जो कहता हूं उसे सुनो

मेरी लड़की बहुत छोटी और मासूम है

उसे बस एक बार जाने दो!

मुसाफिर आया है…

 

प्यारी लड़की के सात भाई,

कोई भी साथ नहीं जा सकता

उसे अभी भी गेम खेलना है

अपने प्यारे दोस्तों के साथ

आपका दामाद आया है...

 

मानसून के दिन आ गए हैं

तीज का त्योहार आ गया है

भाई, दोस्त और परिवार सभी

अपनी लड़की को आशीर्वाद देने आये थे

मेरे पति आये हैं...

 

कहते हैं कबीर, सुनो हे साधकों

इस वैवाहिक घर पर जाएँ

यह वैवाहिक घर हम सभी पर दावा करेगा

गोपियों की तरह इस पर ध्यान करें

आपका दामाद आया है...

 

 

अनुवाद: शबनम विरमानी

songs.about:
Kabir evokes the simultaneous beauty and fragility of our body-selves through a series of metaphors in this song. We are vessels of clay, crumbling at a blow, we are a string of pearls, scattering in an instant...

In June 2010, the Kabir Project team pitched camp in the desert heat of Kutch, Gujarat for an intense 5-day period of day and night recordings with folk and Sufi singers of Kutch, Gujarat. In a late night recording, the feisty and effervescent folk singer Mooralala Marwada, surprised all of us with this gentle and lilting Kabir bhajan. We dropped the dholak, and decided to keep the track light, underscored only by the rhythm of the ghada ghamela...

Mooralala Marwada is the eleventh generation in his family lineage of singers, and has an infectious and irrepressible joy in singing. Though he lives in Khadeer, Kutch, a border district of Gujarat, his ancestry is from the Meghwal community of Rajasthan, and he sings bhajans of Kabir, Meera, Ravidas and other Bhakti poets, and also kaafis of Shah Abdul Latif Bhitai. 

Get Mooralala Marwada's music here.

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
हिए काया में वर्तन माटी रो, हिए देही में

फूटी जासे नहीं करे रनको

साहिब हमको डर लागे एक दिन रो

एक ही रे दिन को, घड़ी पलक रो

नहीं रे भरोसो एक ही पल रो

 

हिए काया में माला मोतियन की, हिए देही में

टूटी जासे डोरो रूढ़ो तन को

साहिब हमको डर लागे एक दिन रो

 

हिए काया में हाट बाजारा, हिए देही में

सौदो करी ले रूढ़ो संत रो

साहिब हमको डर लागे एक दिन रो

 

हिए काया में वाड़ी फूलन की, हिए देही में

मृगा चरी जाए रूढ़ो वन को

साहिब हमको डर लागे एक दिन रो

 

एक दिन आवसे, सबने बुलावसे

लेखा लेसे राई तल को

साहिब हमको डर लागे एक दिन रो

 

कहत कबीरा, सुनो भाई साधो

पहला है नाम अलख रो

साहिब हमको डर लागे एक दिन रो
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Hiye kaaya mein vartan maati ro, hiye dehi mein

Phooti jaase nahin kare ranko

Saahib hamko dar laage ek din ro

Ek hi re din ko, ghadi palak ro

Nahin re bharoso ek hi pal ro

 

Hiye kaaya mein maala motiyan ki, hiye dehi mein

Tooti jaase doro roodhho tan ko

Saahib hamko dar laage ek din ro

 

Hiye kaaya mein haat baajaara, hiye dehi mein

Saudo kari le roodho sant ro

Saahib hamko dar laage ek din ro

 

Hiye kaaya mein vaadi phoolan ki, hiye dehi mein

Mriga chari jaaye roodho van ko

Saahib hamko dar laage ek din ro

 

Ek din aavse, sab ne bulaavse

Lekha lese raai tal ko

Saahib hamko dar laage ek din ro

 

Kahat Kabira, suno bhai saadho

Pehla hai naam alakh ro

Saahib hamko dar laage ek din ro
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
In this body, a vessel of clay
It will shatter
Then never resound
I fear that day, lord
Just one day, one moment
The blink of an eyelid
Can you trust
A single instant?

In this body, a string of pearls
The thread of this body
Will snap one day
I fear that day, lord

In this body, a market bustles
Find a true saint
Strike a good bargain
I fear that day, lord

In this body, a field of flowers
A deer grazes away
The beautiful garden
I fear that day, lord

A day will come
Each one will be called
An account taken
Of every moment
I fear that day, lord

Says Kabir, listen seekers
First and foremost
Remember the invisible
I fear that day, lord

Translation: Vipul Rikhi & Shabnam Virmani

songs.about:
In February 2012 Gavra Devi held several thousands in the village of Diyatara spellbound with her rendition of this folk song, bringing many children and young people to their feet. In this bhajan, the poet Fakru mocks all of us - pointing out how the cart of our awareness, our consciousness, our meditation is wobbling, and urging us to bring it back on track.
 
Gavra Devi is a popular folk singer from Bikaner, evidently well-loved by her audiences who call her 'buaji' (aunt). "Ever since I lost my eyesight as a little girl, I took to bhajan singing and established these poets and their words firmly in my heart," she says.

(Click on Settings for subtitles in English & Hindi.)

This was the 6th night of the week-long Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, a travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in Feb 2012. The Yatra was organized by Lokayan in Bikaner (lokayan.org) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore.

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
भजन रो गुड़क रहयो गाड़ो
कर मालिक ने याद मना
कुण बैठ्यो आड़ो?

पांच वनां री लकड़ी मंगाई
दस वनां री कील
नव दस मास गढ़ंता लागी
चाले लाखूं मील
भजन रो...

ऊँट बड़ो अज्ञान
सत री सांकल घाले
नेम धर्म रा पेटी पटिया
सतगुरू सबदां चाले
भजन रो...

ले भातो घरवाली चाली
प्रेम पूरबलो थाल
हर भजियो गटकावण लाग्यो
हूं आयो तू चाल
भजन रो...

राम नाम रा गेड़ा लाग्या
नित नेम रा नोट
फ़करू केवे कट जासी
थारो जनम जनम रा खोट
भजन रो...
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Bhajan ro gudak rahyo gaado
Kar maalik ne yaad mana
Kun baithhyo aado?

Paanch vanaan ri lakdi mangaayi
Das vanaan ri keel
Nav das maas gadhhanta laagi
Chaale laakhun meel
Bhajan ro…

Oont bado agyaan
Sat ri saankal ghaale
Nem dharm ra peti patiya
Satguru sabdaan chaale
Bhajan ro…

Le bhaato gharvaali chaali
Prem poorablo thaal
Har bhajiyo gatkaavan laagyo
Hun aayo tu chaal
Bhajan ro…

Raam naam ra geda laagya
Nit nem ra not
Fakru keve kat jaasi
Thaaro janam janam ra khot
Bhajan ro…
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Your meditation-cart is tottering
Remember the creator, O heart
Who blocks your path?

Wood from five forests
From ten forests, the pegs
Nine months to fabricate
For a thousand miles it rolls its legs
The cart of meditation…

The camel who pulls it is a fool
Yoke it with the chain of truth
Load it with freight of the Name
The guru’s words chart out the route
The cart of meditation…

Your wife is by your side with food
Served on love’s eastern plate
Gulp it down remembering Hari
Go on, I’m behind you, he says!
The cart of meditation…

Raam’s name, the wares you cart
The eternal Name, your wages
Fakru says thus you will remove
Your knots of endless ages

The cart of meditation…

Translation: Vipul Rikhi

songs.about:
In a radical departure from the norm of pursing wealth and success, in this song the poet Kabir celebrates the joy of poverty, and the beauty of simplicity. But this poverty is not wretched or empty – it is filled with the joy of Raam remembrance. Kabir claims that this joy is far greater than the joys afforded by material comfort or luxury. Saboori, or contentment, is the tool which takes one to fakiri, or the state of being utterly free – free of desires, wants and the incessant need for more!

Kaluram Bamaniya brought this song alive on the 2nd night of the Rajasthan Kabir Yatra in the village of Napasar, bringing some rural women in the audience to their feet. He belongs to a vibrant tradition of singing Bhakti poets such as Kabir, Gorakhnath, Bananath and Meera in the Malwa region of Madhya Pradesh in India.

A special presence on stage in this song is Kaluram ji’s daughter Arpita, singing with gusto while she plays the manjiras. This perhaps best exemplifies how learning happens in the folk traditions – the sounds and styles are imbibed and absorbed through many hours and years of inhabiting shared spaces of satsang, both on and off the stage. Kaluram ji himself starting learning at the early age of 9, by accompanying his father, grandfather and uncle on the manjiras. When he was 13 years old he ran away from home and went to Rajasthan, where he absorbed a wide repertoire of songs from an itinerant Mirasi singer Ram Niwas Rao for 1-2 years. Contact Kaluram Bamaniya at +91-98264-00632 or bairagkabir.bamniya@gmail.com.

Kaluram ji became part of the Kabir Project journeys in 2002 and his music published by us is available here and here. A CD of his songs is also part of Kabir Samagri created by Reena Ginwala available here.

This concert was part of the week-long Rajasthan Kabir Yatra, a travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in February 2012. The Yatra was organised by Lokayan in Bikaner (www.lokayan.org) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore.
