songs.songLyricsOriginal:
मन के मते ना चालिए, मन के मते हैं अनेक
जो मन पर सवार है, सो साधू कोई एक

मन के हारे हार है और मन के जीते जीत
यह मन मिलावे राम से और मन ही करे फ़जीत

मन लागो मेरो यार फ़कीरी में
मन लागो मेरो यार गरीबी में

जो सुख पावेगा नाम (राम) भजन में
वो सुख नाहीं अमीरी में
मन लागो मेरो यार...

हाथ में तुंबा बगल में सोटा
चारों दिशा जागीरी में
मन लागो मेरो यार...

भला बुरा सब का सुन लीजिये
कर गुज़रान गरीबी में
मन लागो मेरो यार...

प्रेम नगर में रहनी हमारी
भली बनी आवे रे सबूरी में
मन लागो मेरो यार...

आखिर यह तन ख़ाक मिलेगा
क्यों फिरे मगरूरी में?
मन लागो मेरो यार...

कहे कबीर सुनो भाई साधो
साहिब मिलेगा सबूरी में
मन लागो मेरो यार...
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Mann ke mate na chaliye, mann ke mate hain anek
Jo mann par savaar hai, so saadhu koi ek

Mann ke haare haar hai aur mann ke jeete jeet
Yeh mann milaave Raam se aur mann hi kare fajeet

Mann laago mero yaar fakiri mein
Mann laago mero yaar gareebi mein

Jo sukh paavega Raam (naam) bhajan mein
Vo sukh naahin ameeri mein
Mann laago mero yaar fakiri mein…

Haath mein tumba bagal mein sota
Chaaron disha jaageeri mein
Mann laago mero yaar fakiri mein…

Bhala bura sab ka sun leejiye
Kar guzraan gareebi mein
Mann laago mero yaar fakiri mein…

Prem nagar mein rehni hamaari
Bhali ban aave re saboori mein
Mann laago mero yaar fakiri mein…

Aakhir yeh tan khaak milega
Kyon phire magroori mein?
Mann laago mero yaar fakiri mein…

Kahe Kabir suno bhai saadho
Saahib milega saboori mein
Mann laago mero yaar fakiri mein…
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
Don’t follow the mind’s ways
The mind has twists and turns
One who can rein the mind
That seeker’s a rare one

Lose to the mind and you lose 
Win over the mind and you win 
This very mind can lead to Raam 
This very mind can lead to ruin

Oh friend, my mind has
taken to living free!

The joy of Raam remembrance
Cannot be found in plenty 

My mind rejoices in poverty
My mind rejoices in simplicity
My mind has taken to living free!

A bowl and a staff is all I carry
Yet my kingdom stretches wherever I see
My mind has taken to living free!

Praise or abuse, listen to it all
But don’t stray from simplicity
My mind has taken to living free!

My dwelling in the city of love
Became beautiful with patience
My mind has taken to living free!

Your body will bite the dust one day
Why strut about, so smug, so vain?
My mind has taken to living free!

Says Kabir, listen seekers
The Lord is found in contentment
My mind has taken to living free!


Translation: Vipul Rikhi & Shabnam Virmani

songs.about:
In this song Kabir evokes the image of our body, our self, being like the five-stringed musical instrument – the tambura. Made of the four elements and containing the fifth element – shoonyata or emptiness – we hold within our self the capacity to resound to a higher sound. But only if we learn to be perfectly strung – not too tight, not too loose, but just so!

The song is beautifully composed and sung in the thumri idiom by Vidya Rao, a performer of thumri-dadra and ghazal, who has studied under the legendary late Naina Devi, Shanti Hiranand and Girija Devi. She also sings songs of Sufi and bhakti poets, verses from Hindu and Buddhist texts and  Islamic forms of naat, soz and nauha.

Vidya has been advisor and friend to the Kabir Project since 2002, and she says, “Singing Kabir in thumri is all about bringing the emptiness of shoonya into the very ornamented form of the thumri. Kabir may be stark and evoking the emptiness (shoonya) of the sky (gagan). But when I think of the way the sky is adorned with the sun, the moon, the rainbow… How can you say it is empty? Emptiness has this adornment. And the adornment is empty. To me they are true, when they are together.”

Contact Vidya Rao at vidyarao19@gmail.com.
Vidya's album is available here.

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
हो साधो
यह तन ठाट तंबूरे का
 
पांच तत्व का बना है तंबूरा
तार लगा नौ तूरे का

यह तन ठाट तंबूरे का

 
ऐंचत तार मरोड़त खूंटी
निकसत राग हजूरे का

यह तन ठाट तंबूरे का

 
टूटा तार बिखर गयी खूंटी
हो गया धूर मधूरे का

यह तन ठाट तंबूरे का

 
या देही का गरब ना कीजै
उड़ गया हंस तंबूरे का

यह तन ठाट तंबूरे का

 
कहें कबीर सुनो भाई साधो
अगम पंथ एक सूरे का

यह तन ठाट तंबूरे का


ठाट = ढांचा, श्रंगार, सजावट 
तूर = एक बाजा, नगाड़ा
ऐंचत = खींचा
खूंटी = लकड़ी की कीली
धूर = धूल
हजूर = खुदा, ईश्वर 
अगम = अगम्य, जिसमें प्रवेश ना हो
सूर = योद्धा, वीर
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Ho saadho

Yeh tan thhaat tamboore ka


Paanch tatva ka bana hai tamboora

Taar laga nau toore ka

Yeh tan...


Ainchat taar marodat khoonti

Niksat raag hajoore ka

Yeh tan...


Toota taar bikhar gayi khoonti

Ho gaya dhoor madhoore ka

Yeh tan...


Ya dehi ka garab na keeje

Ud gaya hans tamboore ka

Yeh tan...


Kahe Kabir suno bhai saadho

Agam panth ek soore ka

Yeh tan...
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
O seekers!

This body is a splendid tambura!


Made of the five elements

Strung together with nine resonances

This body is a splendid tambura!


Tighten the strings, twist the pegs

And it sings the song of the Lord

This body is a splendid tambura!


The strings snap, the pegs lie scattered

The sweetness has turned to dust

This body is a splendid tambura!


Don’t cling in vain to this body

Its swan has flown away

This body is a splendid tambura!


Kabir says, listen seekers

The path of the brave is pathless

This body is a splendid tambura!


Translation: Vidya Rao

songs.about:
When Kabir dances, the planets and stars dance with him! And so does Bindhumalini – who composed this song on a train in a moment of spontaneous inspiration with her guitarist friend Vedanth. And so do we all – who were brought to our feet by the delightful poetic and musical energy of this song!

Bindhu and Vedanth got off that train and sang this freshly minted song to all of us a few days later, captivating audiences at the Rajasthan Kabir Yatra.

Vedanth Bharadwaj is an independent composer, singer and guitarist from Chennai and Bindhumalini Narayanaswamy is a singer and graphic designer based in Bangalore. In their music, the rigour of Carnatic and Hindustani classical music comes together with the western sounds of the guitar with great panache and soul! (sunosuno.in). Contact them at bindhumalini@gmail.com & vedanthmusic@gmail.com.

The Rajasthan Kabir Yatra was a travelling festival which toured 7 villages with 13 singers of Bhakti, Sufi and Baul poetry in February 2012. The Yatra was organized by Lokayan in Bikaner (lokayan.org) in partnership with the Kabir Project, Bangalore.

songs.songLyricsOriginal:
नाचु रे मेरो मन नट होय


ज्ञान के ढोल बजाय रैन दिन

सब्द सुने सब कोई

राहू केतु नवग्रह नाचे

जमपुर आनँद होई

नाचु रे मेरो मन...


छापा तिलक लगाए बाँस चढ़ि

होई रहु जग से न्यारा

सहस कला कर मन मेरो नाचे

रीझे सिरजनहारा

नाचु रे मेरो मन...


जो तुम कूद जाओ भवसागर

कला बदाऊं में तेरो

कहे कबीर, सुनो भाई साधो

हो रहु सतगुरु चेरो

नाचु रे मेरो मन...
songs.songLyricsNotes:
Naachu re mero mann nat hoye

Gyaan ke dhol bajaaye rain din
Sabd sune sab koi
Raahu Ketu navgrah naache
Jampur aanand hoi
Naachu re…

Chhaapa tilak lagaaye baans chadhhi
Hoi rahu jag se nyaara
Sahas kala kar mann mero naache
Reejhe sirjanhaara
Naachu re…

Jo tum kood jaao bhavsaagar
Kala badaaun mein tero
Kahe Kabir, suno bhai saadho
Ho rahu satguru chero
Naachu re…
songs.songLyricsTranslated:
My heart's on its feet
It's become a dancer!
Night and day, day and night
The drums of wisdom beat!

The sound has everyone tuned in
Even planets and stars are swinging
There is joy in the city of death
My heart's on its feet!

Proud of name and status
You strut in front of the world
With a thousand graces
My heart dances
Wooing the creator
My heart's on its feet!

Thrown in the world-ocean
Swim with the skill of stillness
Kabir says, listen seeker
Become a real disciple
My heart is on its feet!


Translation: Vipul Rikhi

films.english_translation: Meera, Who Is Krishna To You After All?
films.about_text: Saint. Lover. Devotee. Rebel. Who was Meera after all? We sense her through her songs. We taste her rage, her love. We feel the power of her radical surrender. And yet Meera, one of the most well-loved saint-poets of India, remains an enigma.

films.about_text: Delving into a Sufi tale about a simple village girl held hostage in the fortress of a powerful king, this film grapples with questions of freedom and imprisonment, longing and belonging. It weaves together the zaahir and the baatin (the apparent and the hidden meanings) of this tale, which is at once spiritual and social, personal and political, historical and timeless, about a woman, her lost homeland and the struggle to remember who we truly are.

films.about_text: Across a landscape of loss and desolation, a woman walks alone, as the howling desert wind, the scorching sun, the moon and mountain, bear witness. As we fall in step with Sasui on her quest for a lost beloved, piercing questions begin to arise - Are seeking and sorrow entwined? How does one abide with suffering? What is the sacrifice that union demands? Bringing to life a Sufi tale from Sindh through the compelling verses of Shah Latif, this film traces an epic journey of separation, awakening, and pursuit.

data.original_text:
स्वांस स्वांस में नाम ले,

विर्था स्वांस मत खोय

ना जाने इन स्वांस का,

आवन होय ना होय

 

स्वांसा की कर सुमिरणी,

ने कर अजपा को जाप

परम तत्व को ध्यान धरो,

सोहंग आपो आप

 

सोहंग पोया पवन में,

ने बांधो सुरत सुमेर

ब्रह्म गांठ ह्रदय धरो,

यही विधि माला फेर

 

माला है निज स्वांस की,

फेरे गा कोई दास

चौरासी भरमे नहीं,

कटे काल की फांस

virtha swaans mat khoye

Na jaane is swaans ka,

aavan hoye na hoye


Swaansa ki kar sumirani,

kar ajapa ko jaap

Param tatva ko dhyaan dharo,

sohang aapo aap


Sohang poya pavan mein,

baandho surat sumer

Brahm gaanthh hirday dharo,

yahi vidhi maala pher


Maala hai nij swaans ki,

pherega koi daas

Chauraasi bharme nahin,

kate kaal ki phaans

data.title: Words knock at the door of a truth that lies beyond them.
data.thumbnail_excerpt: Kabir and Nanak are seekers of the Word. Their path, if they have one, is the path of the Word.
data.interview_about:
“In one sense, Kabir and Nanak are seekers of the Word. Their path, if they have one, is the path of the Word. And the Word is the path by which they bring us to that door, and even knock on it. But before that Truth, that light, can show its face, we are blinded by its brilliance, and we shut our eyes.” – Krishna Nath

Born in 1934 in Varanasi in a family of freedom fighters, Krishna Nathji graduated in economics from Kashi Vidyapith. Subsequently he got deeply involved in socialist movements in newly-independent India, even being imprisoned for his activism on several occasions. At some point, Krishnanathji was deeply attracted to the practice of Buddhist meditation (vipassana), thought and literature, and made a close study of Nagarjuna’s and Vajrayana philosophies. He was one of the Buddhist scholars to have held dialogues with the well-known thinker J Krishnamurti. Author of several books, notably on life and traditions in the Himalayas, he had also edited prestigious journals such as ‘Kalpana’, ‘Mankind’ and ‘Aarthiki’ at different times. Krishnathji used to divide his time between his ‘kuti’ (cottage) near Sarnath and his second home, the Krishnamurti Study Centre at Haridvanam in Bangalore. Krishna Nathji passed away on September 06, 2015

(Click on Settings for subtitles in English.)

Interview Translation: Shabnam Virmani

data.profile:
Abdul Hussain Abdullah Turk (1950-2015), or 'Bawa Bha' as he was fondly called in Kutch, Gujarat, was a man whose heart was steeped in the poetry of the famous 17th century Sufi poet Shah Abdul Latif Bhitai and the sounds of the Kutchi Sindhi raag. He not only understood the nuances of poetry and music of the Sindh region intimately, but also shared it with his audiences in a manner infused with the message of peace, oneness and humanity.


'Bawa Bha' was born on 7th September, 1950, in a family of 10 siblings in a small village called Dhrab near Mundra in Kutch, where he lived till the end of his life. He studied formally upto 7th standard in Mundra's government primary school. It was with his uncle, Ali Mamad Jamadar, a master of the 36 Kutchi raag-raaginis, that Bawa Bha came into his own. Together, they would spend hours at the Rasa Peer dargah near Dhrab immersed in singing beyts, kaafis and raags. A story goes that, somewhere near the dargah, there lived a fakir who Bawa Bha became friends with. He held great knowledge about Sufi shrines, ideas and history. For eight long years Bawa Bha traveled the length and breadth of Kutch with him, visiting several Sufi shrines, until one day the fakir left for Uttar Pradesh.


His uncle put Bawa Bha to work in his fish export business where he handled accounts. Even while working, music stayed with him. He started singing at the young age of 10 and remained completely absorbed in Sufi thought and poetry ever since. The famous Sufi singer of Kutch, Sayyad Kasmasa Abha Miyan, once visited Bawa Bha's village to sing at a wedding. The young Bawa Bha presented his songs in front of the master, and that became his first ‘public performance’. He went on to learn Sindhi music and poetry from Abha Miyan. 


Over the years, his popularity grew, spreading across Kutch. He could not only sing beautifully, but through his renditions also interpret the meanings and the ideas of Sufi poetry for the audience, in clear uncomplicated terms. He played a critical role in reviving the values of Sindhi culture and music in the hearts of his audiences. His discourses on the Kutchi way of life were not limited to music alone. From folk legends to history, Sufism to science, literature to nature, this raconteur could interpret anything for his audience and engage them for hours. His ever-spinning yarn of stories could take you to the depths of your own heart, lift you to great heights, make you fall into the lap of nature, take you on travels to the shrines of Sufi saints or leave you at the door of your Beloved.


Bawa Bha's performance at the 1969 festival called ‘Kutchi Raag Ji Roshnai’, organised by Swami Ramsanghji Rathod, was an important milestone. Later in 1982, Bawa Bha himself organised a festival called ‘Kutch Maldhari Lok Kala Mahotsav’ which brought together folk artists of Khadeer, Banni Pachham, Waagad, Anjaar and Bhuj regions of Kutch. Through the Indian National Theatre he also engaged in research on adivasi (indigenous) song, dance and music, during their three day festival in Chandrapur, Maharashtra. Here he presented his discourse on history in the presence of eminent writers like Kaka Saheb Kalelkar and Makarand Bhai. For his contribution to music, he was awarded the Taramati Award.


Sufism for him was a path towards peace, humanity and brotherhood. He organised the first-ever felicitation of talented artists of Kutch at the Urs (death anniversary) of the famous Sufi saint Haji Peer Baba, and this event subsequently became a lasting tradition. He founded a charitable organisation called Khuddam Sufiyat, which works for the betterment of the society and furthering of Sufi values.


Through his association with All India Radio and other organisations, he helped build a pool of knowledge about the region of Kutch. The Kutchi community, their way of life, music, history, legends, eminent personalities, birds and animals and the unique natural ecosystem of Kutch - Bawa Bha became a channel for this cultural knowledge to travel and spread among different people and places. A guru to several Kutchi singers, he honed their skill and vision while they lived and practiced their art with him for years.


For Bawa Bha himself, it is said that the grace of music came after long tireless days of practice at Rasa Peer's dargah. He passed away in 2015, after four long years of struggling with cancer. The people of Kutch remember Bawa Bha as Ghazi, the fighter.


Adapted and translated by Prashant Parvataneni from a Hindi profile written by Mazharuddin Mutwa & Asif Rayama

data.meta_description: एक वीरान और उजड़े मरुस्थल में, एक औरत अकेली चल रही है - रेगिस्तान की गरजती हवा, जलता हुआ सूरज, चाँद और पहाड़, सब उसकी यात्रा की गवाही देते हैं। जैसे ही हम ससुई के साथ उसके खोए हुए प्रेमी की तलाश में कदम मिलाते हैं, कुछ गहरे सवाल उभरने लगते हैं — क्या तलाश और वेदना आपस में गूँथे हुए हैं? दुख के साथ कैसे जिया जाए? सच्चे मिलन के लिए क्या बलिदान देना पड़ता है? शाह लतीफ़ के कलाम के ज़रिये यह फ़िल्म सिंध की इस सूफ़ी दास्तान का अनुसरण करती है - विरह, तलाश और मिलन की आस की एक अद्भुत यात्रा।
